title: Largest island area
description: Find largest island area in grid

examples.explanation: Grid: 5x4. Step 1: Start at (0,0), value=1, unvisited. DFS from (0,0): Visit (0,0), area=1. Check neighbors: (0,1) land, visit, area=2; (1,0) land, visit, area=3; (1,1) land, visit, area=4. No more connected land. Max_area=4. Step 2: Skip visited cells. Step 3: At (2,3), value=1, unvisited. DFS from (2,3): Visit (2,3), area=1. Check neighbors: (2,4) land, visit, area=2; (3,3) land, visit, area=3; (3,4) land, visit, area=4. Max_area remains 4. Step 4: Return 4.

Grid: 3x5. Step 1: Start at (0,2), value=1, unvisited. DFS from (0,2): Visit (0,2), area=1. Check neighbors: (0,1) water, skip; (0,3) water, skip; (1,2) land, visit, area=2. From (1,2): neighbors (1,1) land, visit, area=3; (1,3) land, visit, area=4; (2,2) land, visit, area=5. Max_area=5. Step 2: No other land cells. Return 5.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS
approaches.summary: Iterate over each cell in the grid, and for each land cell (e.g., value 1), perform a depth-first search (DFS) to compute the area of the connected island, marking visited cells to avoid recounting. This approach is straightforward but can be inefficient for large grids due to repeated traversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Find the maximum area of connected land cells (value 1) in a 2D grid using DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to systematically explore every land cell in the grid, using DFS to compute the area of each connected island while marking visited cells to prevent redundant calculations. This brute-force method ensures we don't miss any islands by checking every cell, but it can be inefficient for large grids due to repeated traversals of visited areas. The algorithm works by iterating through each cell in the grid. For each unvisited land cell, we initiate a DFS to count all connected land cells, updating a global maximum area variable. The DFS recursively explores all four possible directions (up, down, left, right), ensuring we stay within grid bounds and only count unvisited land cells. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n and m are grid dimensions, as each cell is visited at most once. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the recursion stack in worst-case scenarios (e.g., all land cells). This connects to L1 by relying on the DFS subroutine to compute island areas. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark cells as visited before recursion (leading to infinite loops), incorrect boundary checks, or not resetting area counts for each island. Real code pattern: Initialize visited set, max_area=0, loop through grid, if cell is land and unvisited, call DFS to get area, update max_area, return max_area.

examples.trace: Grid: 5x4. Step 1: Start at (0,0), value=1, unvisited. DFS from (0,0): Visit (0,0), area=1. Check neighbors: (0,1) land, visit, area=2; (1,0) land, visit, area=3; (1,1) land, visit, area=4. No more connected land. Max_area=4. Step 2: Skip visited cells. Step 3: At (2,3), value=1, unvisited. DFS from (2,3): Visit (2,3), area=1. Check neighbors: (2,4) land, visit, area=2; (3,3) land, visit, area=3; (3,4) land, visit, area=4. Max_area remains 4. Step 4: Return 4.

Grid: 3x5. Step 1: Start at (0,2), value=1, unvisited. DFS from (0,2): Visit (0,2), area=1. Check neighbors: (0,1) water, skip; (0,3) water, skip; (1,2) land, visit, area=2. From (1,2): neighbors (1,1) land, visit, area=3; (1,3) land, visit, area=4; (2,2) land, visit, area=5. Max_area=5. Step 2: No other land cells. Return 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Area / Union Operation
ladders.desc: Implement a depth-first search function that counts connected land cells from a starting point.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this DFS subroutine is to recursively explore all adjacent land cells from a starting point, accumulating a count to determine the island's area. This works because islands are defined by connected land cells (via up, down, left, right movements), and DFS naturally traverses such connected components by following paths until no more land is found, then backtracking. The algorithm: Define a recursive function dfs(row, col) that takes grid coordinates. Base case: If out of bounds or cell is water (0) or already visited, return 0. Otherwise, mark cell as visited, set area = 1 (for current cell), then recursively call dfs on all four neighbors, summing their areas. Return total area. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of cells in the island, as each cell is visited once. Space complexity is O(k) for recursion depth in worst case (e.g., a long line of land cells). This feeds into L0 by providing the core mechanism to compute area for each island discovered in the main loop. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (causing infinite loops), missing boundary checks, or incorrect neighbor coordinates. Real code pattern: def dfs(grid, row, col, visited): if out of bounds or grid[row][col]==0 or (row,col) in visited: return 0; visited.add((row,col)); area=1; for each direction: area+=dfs(grid, new_row, new_col, visited); return area.

examples.input: Grid: [[1,1],[1,0]], Start: (0,0), Visited: empty
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0,0): In bounds, land, unvisited. Mark visited. area=1. Step 2: Check neighbors: (0,1) land, call dfs(0,1): Mark visited, area=1; neighbors: (0,0) visited, skip; (0,2) out of bounds; (1,1) water; (-1,1) out of bounds. Return 1 to (0,0). area=1+1=2. Step 3: Neighbor (1,0) land, call dfs(1,0): Mark visited, area=1; neighbors: (1,1) water; (1,-1) out of bounds; (0,0) visited; (2,0) out of bounds. Return 1 to (0,0). area=2+1=3. Step 4: Neighbor (-1,0) out of bounds. Return 3.

Step 1: dfs(1,1): Land, unvisited. Mark visited. area=1. Step 2: Check neighbors: (1,0) land, call dfs(1,0): Mark visited, area=1; neighbors: (1,-1) out of bounds; (1,1) visited; (0,0) water; (2,0) water. Return 1. area=1+1=2. Step 3: Neighbor (1,2) land, call dfs(1,2): Mark visited, area=1; neighbors: (1,3) out of bounds; (1,1) visited; (0,2) water; (2,2) water. Return 1. area=2+1=3. Step 4: Neighbor (0,1) land, call dfs(0,1): Mark visited, area=1; neighbors: (0,0) water; (0,2) water; (-1,1) out of bounds; (1,1) visited. Return 1. area=3+1=4. 
... (simplified)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Visited Marking / Size Tracking
ladders.desc: Understand and implement a mechanism to track visited cells during traversal.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind marking visited cells is to prevent infinite loops and redundant calculations when traversing connected components in a grid. In DFS, without marking, we might revisit the same cell multiple times, leading to incorrect area counts or stack overflow. This works by maintaining a data structure (e.g., a set or a separate grid) that records which cells have been processed, ensuring each cell contributes to the area only once. The algorithm: When entering a cell during DFS, check if it's already in the visited set; if so, skip it. Otherwise, add it to the visited set before exploring neighbors. This ensures that once a cell is counted, it won't be recounted in the same or different DFS calls. Time complexity for marking is O(1) per cell on average for set operations. Space complexity is O(n*m) in worst case if all cells are visited. This connects to L1 by being an essential part of the DFS subroutine to ensure correctness. Common mistakes include marking visited after recursion (which can cause loops), using a local visited set per DFS call (losing global state), or not clearing visited between problems. Real code pattern: visited = set(); in dfs: if (row,col) in visited: return 0; visited.add((row,col)); proceed with recursion.

examples.input: Grid: [[1,1],[1,1]], Start DFS from (0,0) with visited set
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0,0): (0,0) not in visited, add. area=1. Step 2: Check neighbor (0,1): not visited, call dfs(0,1): add to visited, area=1; neighbors: (0,0) visited, skip; others out of bounds/water. Return 1. area=1+1=2. Step 3: Check neighbor (1,0): not visited, call dfs(1,0): add to visited, area=1; neighbors: (1,1) not visited, call dfs(1,1): add to visited, area=1; neighbors all visited or out of bounds. Return 1 to (1,0). area=1+1=2 at (1,0). Return 2 to (0,0). area=2+2=4. Visited set contains all four cells.

examples.input: Grid: [[1,0,1],[0,1,0],[1,0,1]], Start DFS from (0,0) with visited set
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0,0): not visited, add. area=1. Step 2: Check neighbors: (0,1) water, skip; (0,-1) out of bounds; (1,0) water, skip; (-1,0) out of bounds. Return 1. Visited set only has (0,0), preventing recount if other DFS calls occur.

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal
ladders.desc: Learn to iterate over a 2D grid and safely access neighbors with bounds checking.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for grid traversal with boundary checks is to systematically explore a 2D array while avoiding errors from accessing indices outside the grid's dimensions. This is fundamental in problems involving matrices or grids, as operations often require examining adjacent cells. It works by defining the grid's rows and columns, then using loops or recursion to visit cells, and before accessing any neighbor, verifying that its coordinates are within [0, rows-1] and [0, cols-1]. The algorithm: Let rows = len(grid), cols = len(grid[0]). To traverse, use nested loops for row in range(rows) and col in range(cols). For neighbor checks, define directions = [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)] representing up, down, left, right. For each direction, compute new_row = row + dr, new_col = col + dc, then check if 0 <= new_row < rows and 0 <= new_col < cols before proceeding. Time complexity for full traversal is O(rows*cols). Space complexity is O(1) excluding input storage. This connects to L2 by providing the framework to apply visited marking during traversal. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in bounds (e.g., using <= instead of <), missing checks for negative indices, or incorrect direction arrays. Real code pattern: rows, cols = len(grid), len(grid[0]); for r in range(rows): for c in range(cols): # process cell; for dr, dc in directions: nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc; if 0 <= nr < rows and 0 <= nc < cols: # access grid[nr][nc].

examples.input: Grid: 2x3 [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], Traverse and print each cell and its valid neighbors
examples.trace: Step 1: rows=2, cols=3. Step 2: For row=0, col=0: value=1. Check neighbors: (-1,0) out of bounds; (1,0) in bounds, value=4; (0,-1) out of bounds; (0,1) in bounds, value=2. Step 3: For row=0, col=1: value=2. Neighbors: (-1,1) out of bounds; (1,1) in bounds, value=5; (0,0) in bounds, value=1; (0,2) in bounds, value=3. Continue for all cells.

examples.input: Grid: 1x1 [[9]], Check neighbors for cell (0,0)
examples.trace: Step 1: rows=1, cols=1. Step 2: For row=0, col=0: value=9. Check directions: (-1,0): new_row=-1, out of bounds; (1,0): new_row=1, out of bounds; (0,-1): new_col=-1, out of bounds; (0,1): new_col=1, out of bounds. All neighbors invalid.

testCases.expected: [(1,0)=3, (3,0) out of bounds, (2,-1) out of bounds, (2,1)=6]
[(0,2)=1, (2,2) out of bounds, (1,1)=1, (1,3) out of bounds]

testCases.input: Grid: [[5]], Check if (0,0) has neighbor up

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Cell Access / Index Mapping
ladders.desc: Learn how to read from and write to cells in a two-dimensional array (grid).
ladders.explanation: The intuition for accessing and modifying 2D array elements is that a grid is represented as a list of lists in many programming languages, where each inner list corresponds to a row, and indices specify row and column positions. This operation is fundamental because most grid-based algorithms require checking cell values (e.g., land vs water) or updating them (e.g., marking visited). It works by using double indexing: grid[row][col] to get or set the value at that position. Ensure indices are integers and within bounds to avoid errors. The algorithm: To read a value, simply use grid[row][col]. To modify, assign a new value: grid[row][col] = new_value. Always validate row and col are within 0 and len(grid)-1 for rows, and 0 and len(grid[0])-1 for columns. Time complexity is O(1) for access/modification. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This connects to L3 by being the core action performed during grid traversal and neighbor checks. Common mistakes include confusing row and column order (e.g., grid[col][row]), using floats as indices, or accessing out-of-bounds indices leading to runtime errors. Real code pattern: value = grid[row][col]; grid[row][col] = 0; # example modification to mark as visited by setting to 0.

examples.input: Grid: [[1,2],[3,4]], Access cell (0,1), then set it to 9
examples.output: Initial value: 2, After modification: grid becomes [[1,9],[3,4]]
examples.trace: Step 1: grid is a 2x2 list. Step 2: Access grid[0][1]: row=0, col=1, value=2. Step 3: Modify: grid[0][1] = 9. Step 4: Verify: grid[0] is now [1,9], grid[1] remains [3,4].

examples.input: Grid: [[5,6,7],[8,9,10]], Access cell (1,2), then set it to 0
examples.output: Initial value: 10, After modification: grid becomes [[5,6,7],[8,9,0]]
examples.trace: Step 1: grid has 2 rows, 3 cols. Step 2: Access grid[1][2]: row=1, col=2, value=10. Step 3: Modify: grid[1][2] = 0. Step 4: Check: grid[1] is now [8,9,0].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Grids & Connected Components
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of 2D grids and connected components of land cells.
ladders.explanation: In a grid, land cells (1) connected via 4-directions form islands. The largest island area is the maximum number of connected land cells. DFS or Union-Find efficiently finds these components.

examples.input: Represent a 2x3 grid with values: row0: 1,2,3; row1: 4,5,6
examples.trace: Step 1: Define grid as a list. Step 2: First inner list: [1,2,3] for row 0. Step 3: Second inner list: [4,5,6] for row 1. Step 4: Result is a list containing two lists: [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]. Number of rows: 2, columns: 3.

examples.input: Given grid = [[7,8],[9,10],[11,12]], find number of rows and columns, and access element at row 2, column 0
examples.trace: Step 1: grid is a list of lists: [[7,8],[9,10],[11,12]]. Step 2: Count rows: len(grid) = 3. Step 3: Count columns: len(grid[0]) = 2 (all rows have same length). Step 4: Access grid[2][0]: row index 2 refers to third list [11,12], column index 0 refers to first element 11.

testCases.input: Given grid = [[1,2,3]], find rows and columns
Given grid = [[0],[0],[0]], access cell at row 1, col 0
Represent a 2x2 grid with values: top-left 9, top-right 8, bottom-left 7, bottom-right 6
Given grid = [[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]], find number of rows
Given grid = [[10,20,30],[40,50,60]], access cell at row 0, col 2
Given grid = [[1]], access cell at row 0, col 0
Represent a 1x3 grid with values a,b,c

approaches.name: Union-Find
approaches.summary: Use a disjoint set data structure to union adjacent land cells and track the size of each connected component dynamically. After processing all cells, find the largest component size. This method is efficient for incremental updates and avoids explicit graph traversal for each cell.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution using Union-Find to find the largest connected land area in a grid.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind using Union-Find for this problem is that we need to efficiently group adjacent land cells (1s) into connected components while tracking their sizes. Union-Find provides O(α(n)) amortized time for union and find operations, making it ideal for this grid connectivity problem. The algorithm works by treating each land cell as an individual component initially, then unioning adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) that are both land. We maintain size information in the root nodes to quickly determine component sizes. After processing all cells, we find the maximum size among all components. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize Union-Find structures: parent array (each cell points to itself), size array (all land cells start with size 1, water cells 0). 2) Iterate through grid: for each land cell, check its four neighbors; if neighbor is land and in bounds, union current cell with neighbor. 3) After processing all unions, iterate through all cells to find maximum size among land cells' root components. Time complexity: O(n*m*α(n*m)) where α is inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant). Space: O(n*m) for parent and size arrays. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the union operation (L1) as its key subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle water cells (size 0), not using path compression or union by size (causing poor performance), incorrectly handling grid boundaries. Real code pattern: class DSU with parent, size arrays; find with path compression; union by size; main function iterates grid, unions adjacent lands, returns max size.

examples.trace:
Grid: 4x5. Initialize DSU: parent[0..19], size[0..19]. Process cell (0,0)=1: neighbors (0,1)=1 (union: root0+root1, size=2), (1,0)=1 (union: root0+root2, size=3). Cell (0,1)=1: already unioned. Cell (1,0)=1: already unioned. Cell (1,1)=1: neighbors (0,1)=1 (union: root1+root3, but root1 already in component0, size=4). Continue scanning: cells (2,3)=1: neighbors (2,4)=1 (union: root13+root14, size=2), (3,3)=1 (union: root13+root18, size=3), (3,4)=1 (union: root14+root19, but root14 already in component13, size=4). Final components: component0 si
... (simplified)

Grid: 8x13. Initialize DSU. Process land cells: multiple unions form components. Key unions: cells around (3,8)-(4,10) form large component: (3,8)=1 union with (3,9)=1, (4,8)=1, (4,9)=1, (4,10)=1, (5,10)=1. This component size becomes 6. Other components: top-right island size=4 (cells around (0,7)-(1,9)), left island size=3 (cells (2,1),(3,1),(4,1)), etc. After all unions, max size = 6 from the central component.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Area / Union Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the union operation that merges two components and updates their total size.
ladders.explanation: The union operation is the heart of the Union-Find approach for this problem. Intuition: when we discover two adjacent land cells, we need to merge their components into one island and keep track of the combined size. We do this by finding the roots of both cells (using find operation), and if they're different, we attach the smaller component's root to the larger one's root (union by size/rank) to keep the tree flat. We then update the size of the new root to be the sum of both component sizes. This ensures that after union, we can quickly query the size of any component by looking at its root's size. Step-by-step: 1) Find root of cell x (rx) and root of cell y (ry). 2) If rx == ry, they're already connected, do nothing. 3) Else, if size[rx] < size[ry], attach rx to ry and update size[ry] += size[rx]. 4) Otherwise, attach ry to rx and update size[rx] += size[ry]. Time: O(α(n)) amortized due to path compression in find. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This union operation is called repeatedly in the main algorithm to build islands. Connection to L2: This uses find operation (L2) as a subroutine. Common mistakes: not checking if roots are same (causing cycles), not updating size correctly, not using union by size (leading to unbalanced trees). Real code pattern: def union(x, y): rx, ry = find(x), find(y); if rx != ry: if size[rx] < size[ry]: parent[rx] = ry; size[ry] += size[rx]; else: parent[ry] = rx; size[rx] += size[ry].

examples.input: Initial: parent=[0,1,2,3], size=[1,1,1,1]. Union cells 0 and 1.
examples.trace: Find root of 0: 0 (parent[0]=0). Find root of 1: 1 (parent[1]=1). Roots different (0≠1). Compare sizes: size[0]=1, size[1]=1 (equal). Choose to attach root1 to root0 (or vice versa). Attach 1 to 0: parent[1]=0. Update size[0] += size[1] = 1+1=2. Result: parent=[0,0,2,3], size=[2,1,1,1] (note: size[1] is now irrelevant as it's not a root).

examples.input: Initial: parent=[0,0,2,3,4], size=[2,1,1,1,1]. Union cells 2 and 4.
examples.trace: Find root of 2: 2 (parent[2]=2). Find root of 4: 4 (parent[4]=4). Roots different (2≠4). Compare sizes: size[2]=1, size[4]=1 (equal). Attach 2 to 4: parent[2]=4. Update size[4] += size[2] = 1+1=2. Result: parent=[0,0,4,3,4], size=[2,1,1,1,2]. Now union cells 0 and 4: find root0=0, root4=4, sizes: size[0]=2, size[4]=2 (equal). Attach 4 to 0: parent[4]=0. Update size[0] += size[4] = 2+2=4. Final: parent=[0,0,4,3,0], size=[4,1,1,1,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Visited Marking / Size Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation that locates the root of a component and compresses the path.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is fundamental to Union-Find efficiency. Intuition: each cell belongs to a component represented by a root node; find returns this root while flattening the tree (path compression) to make future finds faster. We recursively follow parent pointers until reaching a node that is its own parent (the root). During backtracking, we set each visited node's parent directly to the root, compressing the path. This ensures near-constant time amortized operations. Step-by-step: 1) If parent[x] == x, return x (base case: x is root). 2) Else, recursively find root = find(parent[x]). 3) Set parent[x] = root (path compression). 4) Return root. Time: O(α(n)) amortized due to compression. Space: O(1) extra, but recursion uses stack space O(log n) worst-case without tail recursion optimization; iterative version avoids this. Connection to L1: find is used within union to get roots. Connection to L3: this relies on the parent array (L3) structure. Common mistakes: not implementing path compression (causing O(n) time), infinite recursion if parent pointers corrupted, not handling base case correctly. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x].

examples.input: parent=[1,2,2,3,4]. Find root of 0.
examples.trace: Start at 0: parent[0]=1≠0, so find(1). At 1: parent[1]=2≠1, so find(2). At 2: parent[2]=2, so root=2. Backtrack: set parent[1]=2. Backtrack: set parent[0]=2. Return 2. Final parent array: [2,2,2,3,4].

examples.input: parent=[0,0,1,2,3]. Find root of 4.
examples.trace: Start at 4: parent[4]=3≠4, find(3). At 3: parent[3]=2≠3, find(2). At 2: parent[2]=1≠2, find(1). At 1: parent[1]=0≠1, find(0). At 0: parent[0]=0, root=0. Backtrack: set parent[1]=0. Backtrack: set parent[2]=0. Backtrack: set parent[3]=0. Backtrack: set parent[4]=0. Return 0. Final parent: [0,0,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the parent array that represents the forest of trees in Union-Find.
ladders.explanation: The parent array is the core data structure in Union-Find. Intuition: we represent each element (grid cell) as a node in a forest of trees, where each tree is a connected component. The parent array stores, for each node, the index of its parent node. A root node points to itself. Initially, each cell is its own component, so parent[i] = i for all i. When we union two components, we make one root point to the other root, merging the trees. This array allows efficient find operations by following parent pointers. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize parent array of length n (where n = rows*cols for grid). 2) Set parent[i] = i for all i. 3) During union, update parent[root_of_smaller] = root_of_larger. 4) During find, traverse parent pointers to root. Time: initialization O(n). Space: O(n). Connection to L2: the find operation traverses this array. Connection to L4: indexing into this array is a basic operation. Common mistakes: not initializing properly (leading to incorrect roots), using wrong indices for grid cells (need to map 2D to 1D), not updating parent correctly during union. Real code pattern: parent = [i for i in range(rows*cols)].

examples.input: Grid 2x2, initialize parent array.
examples.trace: Grid has 4 cells. Map cell (r,c) to index i = r*cols + c. So: (0,0)->0, (0,1)->1, (1,0)->2, (1,1)->3. Initialize parent[i] = i for i=0..3. Result: [0,1,2,3].

examples.input: After unions: parent initially [0,1,2,3]. Union cell0 and cell1, then union cell2 and cell3.
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3]. Union(0,1): find root0=0, root1=1, attach 1 to 0: parent[1]=0. Now parent=[0,0,2,3]. Union(2,3): find root2=2, root3=3, attach 3 to 2: parent[3]=2. Final parent=[0,0,2,2].

testCases.input: parent=[0,1,1,3]; find and compress path for 0
parent=[0,0,1,2]; find and compress path for 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Cell Access / Index Mapping
ladders.desc: Convert between 2D grid coordinates and 1D array indices for Union-Find operations.
ladders.explanation: In Union-Find for grid problems, we need to represent each cell as an element in our arrays. Intuition: a 2D grid with rows and cols can be flattened into a 1D array of size rows*cols. This mapping allows us to use simple array indices for parent and size arrays. The formula: index = r * cols + c, where r is row (0-indexed), c is column (0-indexed). Conversely, given index i, we can compute r = i // cols, c = i % cols. This is a basic but crucial operation to avoid off-by-one errors and ensure correct cell referencing. Step-by-step: 1) Determine rows and cols of grid. 2) For each cell (r,c), compute i = r*cols + c. 3) Use i to access parent[i], size[i]. 4) When checking neighbors, compute their indices using the same formula, ensuring they're within bounds (0 ≤ r < rows, 0 ≤ c < cols). Time: O(1) per mapping. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: this mapping is used to initialize and access the parent array. Connection to L5: relies on understanding arrays and grid coordinates. Common mistakes: using wrong formula (e.g., r*rows + c), not handling bounds, forgetting that indices start at 0. Real code pattern: def get_index(r, c, cols): return r * cols + c.

examples.input: Grid 3x4, cell (1,2).
Grid 2x5, index 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Grids & Connected Components
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of 2D grids and connected components of land cells.
ladders.explanation: In a grid, land cells (1) connected via 4-directions form islands. The largest island area is the maximum number of connected land cells. DFS or Union-Find efficiently finds these components.

examples.trace: Grid has 2 rows, 3 cols. Row indices: 0,1. Col indices: 0,1,2. Cell (1,1) means row=1, col=1. grid[1][1] = 1. Check: grid[1] is second row [0,1,0]; index 1 gives 1.

examples.input: Same grid, get neighbors of (0,2).
examples.output: Up: out of bounds, Down: (1,2)=0, Left: (0,1)=0, Right: out of bounds
examples.trace: Cell (0,2): row=0, col=2. Directions: up (-1,0): ( -1,2) row out of bounds (<0). Down (1,0): (1,2) within bounds? rows=2, so row=1 valid, col=2 valid; grid[1][2]=0. Left (0,-1): (0,1) valid; grid[0][1]=0. Right (0,1): (0,3) col out of bounds (cols=3, so max col=2). So valid neighbors: down and left.
